lesson.menu_desc: Past regrets & the third conditional -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- Looking Back, Moving Forward
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 17 -- Looking Back, Moving Forward

extra_audio.text: First, Pricila looks back on her short trip to Italy. In hindsight, she wishes she had stayed the full month. Then she thinks: if she had stayed, she would have learned the language. After that, she realizes it was a turning point that shaped her life. So she lets it go with no regrets and looks forward.

listenings.text: There's one trip I still think about, even now. Years ago, I had the chance to spend a whole month travelling through the south of Italy, slowly, with no fixed plan. But I was nervous about being away from work for so long, so I cut it down to ten days and rushed through everything. In hindsight, I wish I had trusted myself more. If I had taken the full month, I would have learned the language properly, I would have made real friends, and I would have come home a different person. If only I had known then what I know now. And yet, I try not to dwell on it. That short trip was still a turning point. It taught me that I love to travel slowly, and it gave me the courage to plan the long journeys I take today. So when I look back on it, the regret is real, but it is gentle. If I had not made that mistake, I would not have understood what truly matters to me. In a strange way, it all came full circle.

Hey Pricila, it's me. I got your message about looking back on the old days, and I wanted to leave you a quick thought. You asked if I have any regrets about all the trips we never took. Honestly? Not really. Sure, if I had been braver when I was younger, I would have seen more of the world by now. If I had saved a little more, I would have gone to Japan when we first talked about it twenty years ago. But here is the thing: I live by no regrets. Every choice I made, good or bad, brought me to a life I am happy with. I'd rather make the most of the road ahead than second-guess the road behind me. So my advice, traveller to traveller, is this: take stock of what you have learned, but don't dwell on the doors you didn't open. Look back just long enough to smile, and then look forward. The best journey is always the next one. Talk soon.

reading.rtitle: The road not taken -- and the one that was
paras: Every traveller carries a small museum of roads not taken. There is the flight you almost booked, the city you walked past, the invitation you politely declined because the timing felt wrong. Looking back, it is tempting to believe that the other choice would have been the better one -- that if you had only been braver, the whole story would have unfolded more beautifully. The third conditional is the grammar of this quiet daydream: if I had gone, I would have seen; if I had stayed, I would have missed. It lets us replay the past in a key it never actually played.

And yet there is a trap in it. To dwell endlessly on the missed turning is to live in a journey that never happened, while the real one slips past unnoticed. The healthiest travellers, it seems, are not the ones with no regrets at all, but the ones who hold their regrets lightly. They take stock of what a wrong turn taught them. They notice that the cancelled trip led, by some strange route, to a better one. They look back just long enough to learn, and then they turn around and face the road ahead.

Perhaps that is the real lesson of the third conditional. It is not a tool for punishing yourself with everything you would have done differently. It is a way of making peace: if I had not taken that imperfect path, I would not have arrived exactly here, with exactly this view. Hindsight, used gently, does not weigh you down. It hands you a map for the next departure -- and reminds you that the best journey is almost always the one you have not taken yet.

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main message about looking back on the past?
choices: You should carefully analyse every wrong decision so that you never make a mistake on a trip again.
Reflecting on the past is healthy when you hold regrets lightly -- you learn from them, make peace, and turn to face the road ahead.
The third conditional proves that the choices we did not make would always have been better than the ones we did.

items: The writer says every traveller remembers some roads and choices they did not take.
t
Every traveller carries a small museum of roads not taken.
According to the writer, the healthiest travellers are the ones who have no regrets at all.
f
They are not the ones with no regrets, but the ones who hold their regrets lightly.
The writer warns that dwelling endlessly on a missed turning makes you miss the real journey.
t
To dwell endlessly on the missed turning is to live in a journey that never happened, while the real one slips past.
The writer thinks the third conditional should be used to punish yourself for past mistakes.
f
It is not a tool for punishing yourself; it is a way of making peace.

matching.title: Match each reflection expression to its meaning
defs: Looking back now, with the understanding you did not have at the time
A moment when an important change of direction happens in your life
To think too much, and for too long, about something in the past
The feeling of being at peace with your choices, good or bad
To use a situation or chance as fully and well as you possibly can
To return, after a long journey, to where you started or to an old idea
To pause and think carefully about your situation and what you have learned
A bold decision to do something without knowing how it will turn out

rephrase.title: Rewrite each idea using the third conditional
items: Fact: I didn't book early, so I didn't get a good price. (regret it)
If I had booked early, I would have got a good price.
Fact: I didn't learn English sooner. (use 'I wish')
I wish I had learned English sooner.
Fact: She didn't take that trip. (use 'if only')
If only she had taken that trip.
Fact: We didn't save money, so we couldn't go to Japan.
If we had saved money, we could have gone to Japan.
Fact: I made that mistake, and it led me here. (a positive 3rd conditional)
If I hadn't made that mistake, I wouldn't have arrived here.

answerkey.title: Reveal the third-conditional answer key
list: If I had booked early, I would have got a good price.
I wish I had learned English sooner.
If only she had taken that trip.
If we had saved money, we could have gone to Japan.
If I hadn't made that mistake, I wouldn't have arrived here.
answerkey.note: Third conditional = an unreal PAST: if + past perfect (had + past participle), would/could have + past participle. The past perfect carries the 'unreal' meaning, so NEVER put 'would' in the if-clause (NOT 'if I would have booked'). 'I wish' and 'if only' + past perfect express the same past regret: I wish I had... / If only I had...

analyse.title: Close reading
items: The writer calls the third conditional 'the grammar of this quiet daydream'. What does that metaphor mean, and why is the third conditional the right tense for looking back?
The writer says the healthiest travellers 'hold their regrets lightly'. What is the difference between learning from a regret and dwelling on it?

In the last paragraph, the third conditional is used in a positive way: 'if I had not taken that imperfect path, I would not have arrived exactly here.' How does that one sentence turn a regret into peace?
